mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar F-Type 3.0 V6 S engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned F-Type V6 S (306PS AJ126) engine typically costs £5,000–£11,000 supply only depending on the power output and year. One specialist offers the engine for £4,295 supply only with fitting at £1,450. JLR have quoted £17,000 for a new engine, with a 50% goodwill contribution reducing the bill to £8,500. Reconditioning or rebuilding is often in the £8,000–£10,000 range. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar F-Type 3.0 V6 S engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The AJ126 is a great engine, but it has known vulnerabilities. The plastic cooling system components and water pump are major weak points. The timing setup issue is well known — the tensioners wear a hole in the guides, typically around 100,000 miles. The supercharger coupler can fail, causing a loud rattle. The AJ126 has the same tensioner and timing chain problems as the AJ133 V8, just not as bad or frequently. With meticulous maintenance (oil changes every 8,000 miles), these engines can be reliable.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar F-Type 3.0 V6 S?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cooling system failures (plastic V-pipe under the supercharger, water pump), supercharger coupler wear (rattling on cold start), and timing chain tensioner wear (rattle on cold start). The cooling system is the most common and urgent issue. Upgraded aluminium parts are available for the supercharged engine.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Jaguar F-Type 3.0 V6 S engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 60% Jaguar scrap threshold: if your F-Type is worth £20,000–£30,000 and a recon engine is £8,000 (27–40%), it's worth it. If the vehicle is a tired £15,000 example, the same engine exceeds 50% — get a scrap valuation first. These are performance vehicles that often justify replacement spend if the rest of the car is in good condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Jaguar F-Type 3.0 V6 S engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The F-Type V6 S uses the 306PS (also known as the AJ126) — a 3.0-litre supercharged V6. Early cars (2014–2016) produce 380 bhp, while later cars (2016–2020) produce 400 bhp. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the cooling system, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cooling system components can be repaired individually — V-pipe, water pump, thermostat. Upgraded aluminium parts are available for the supercharged engine. However, ignoring coolant warnings can lead to catastrophic engine failure, requiring a full replacement. Address any coolant leaks immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Jaguar F-Type 3.0 V6 S Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current market rates. One specialist offers a Jaguar F-Type S 3.0 V6 engine for £4,295 supply only, with fitting at £1,450. JLR have quoted £17,000 for a new engine, with a 50% goodwill contribution reducing the bill to £8,500 — but that's still a significant figure for a 5-year-old car. Reconditioning or rebuilding is often in the £8,000–£10,000 range.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an F-Type V6 S engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The tightly packed engine bay and complex supercharger system add significant labour time.

cta.label: → Compare F-Type 3.0 V6 S engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Cooling System Failures — Plastic V-Pipe & Water Pump
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, white smoke from engine bay, visible coolant leak, intermittent coolant warnings. The plastic V-pipe under the supercharger is a well-known weak spot that often fails catastrophically.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; ignoring coolant warnings can lead to catastrophic engine failure.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if the coolant warning comes on, pull over immediately.

problems.recommendation: The cooling system is the Achilles' heel of the AJ126. The plastic V-pipe under the supercharger is notorious for cracking. The water pump is also a weak point. Upgraded aluminium parts are available for the supercharged engine. If you see any coolant warnings or smell coolant, have the system inspected immediately — ignoring these signs can lead to warped cylinder heads or full engine failure.

problems.title: Supercharger Coupler Wear
problems.symptoms: A loud rattle from the top of the engine on cold start — like "marbles in a tin can". The rattle is irregular and non-rhythmical, fading in and out. The noise often reduces as the engine warms up.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if left for many thousands of miles, supercharger performance will degrade.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if you hear rattling.

problems.recommendation: The supercharger coupler is a known wear item on the AJ126. The good news is that a worn coupler is normally nothing to worry about and is just annoying. However, if left for many thousands of miles, it will eventually wear so much that supercharger performance will degrade. If you hear the characteristic "marbles in a tin can" rattle on cold start, have the coupler inspected.

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
problems.symptoms: Rattling timing chains — more regular and higher pitched than a coupler rattle, worse on cold start. The tensioners wear a hole in the guides. Can eventually lead to a stretched or skipped chain, causing pistons to hit valves.
problems.typicalMileage: 100,000+ miles — normally they go near 100k miles, though some owners have reported issues at 47,000–51,000 miles.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if the chain snaps, it's an interference engine — catastrophic failure requiring full engine replacement.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: at first sign of metallic rattle on cold start, book inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The AJ126 has the same tensioner/timing chain problems as the AJ133 5.0-litre V8, just not as bad or frequently and not until much higher mileage. The AJ126 is nowhere near as bad for timing chain failure as the AJ133 was in the early days. To prevent timing chain issues, change the engine oil far more often than the scheduled interval — roughly every 8,000 miles. If you hear a regular, high-pitched rattle on cold start, book an inspection immediately.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: F-Type 3.0 V6 S — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential repair — catch early to avoid overheating
Preventative — catches fault before catastrophic failure
Interference engine — piston-valve contact means little is left to repair
Viable if the rest of the vehicle is solid; often cheaper to fit a good used engine

buyingChecks.title: Buying This F-Type V6 S? Check These First
buyIf: * Cooling system recently refreshed (V-pipe, water pump, thermostat) — the most critical maintenance item
* No coolant warnings or evidence of coolant leaks
* Full service history with oil changes every 8,000 miles or less
* Supercharger coupler inspected and confirmed healthy
avoidIf: * Coolant warning lights or evidence of leaks — cooling system issues are common and can be catastrophic
* Rattling from the engine — could be timing chain or supercharger coupler wear
* Poor service history — these engines are sensitive to oil quality
* Overheating or coolant loss — can cause warped cylinder heads or full engine failure

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The AJ126 is a great engine when properly maintained, but its cooling system requires attention and it's sensitive to oil quality. The plastic cooling components are well-known weak points. Coolant warnings should never be ignored. An £8,000–£10,000 reconditioned engine on an F-Type valued at £20,000–£30,000 is 27–50% of the car's value — worth it for a well-maintained example. On a sub-£15,000 example, think carefully. The 60% Jaguar scrap threshold applies.

cta.label: → Compare F-Type V6 S engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Jaguar F-Type 3.0 V6 S (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Coolant Level — Check for low coolant or visible leaks — the most common issue
* Coolant Warning — Any intermittent "Coolant Level Low" warnings?
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling on startup (timing chain or supercharger coupler wear)
* Supercharger Noise — Any "marbles in a tin can" rattle on cold start?
* Service History — Oil changes every 8,000 miles — the AJ126 is sensitive to oil quality
* Engine Bay — Check for coolant residue or crusted coolant on hoses and around the supercharger
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
